steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The subject is Olympia Brown of Connecticut (a single noun phrase: name + origin). The verb that follows must complete this subject without splitting it: "Olympia Brown of Connecticut played an important role."

steps.title: Apply the rule
steps.content:
Identify the structure: "Olympia Brown of [BLANK]" is a subject noun phrase; the blank fills the end of that phrase, and the verb "played" follows. No punctuation should separate a subject from its verb.
  • Choice A — Connecticut played: "Olympia Brown of Connecticut played an important role..." — subject flows directly into verb. ✓
  • Choice B — Connecticut; played: "Olympia Brown of Connecticut; played an important role..." — semicolon splits subject from verb, creating a fragment before and after. ✗
  • Choice C — Connecticut: played: "Olympia Brown of Connecticut: played an important role..." — colon splits subject from verb; colons introduce lists or explanations, not verbs. ✗
  • Choice D — Connecticut. Played: "Olympia Brown of Connecticut. Played an important role..." — period creates a sentence fragment ("Played an important role...") with no subject. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
